9 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

JavaScript objects are everywhere in test automation.
You use them to store:

• Environment URLs

• API request payloads

• API responses

• Test configuration

• User credentials

• Browser settings

• Feature flags

• Expected test data

• Database records
Objects store data as key-value pairs.
For example:
const envUrls = {
DEV: "https://dev.app.com",
STAGE: "https://stage.app.com",
PROD: "https://app.com"
};Here:
DEV
STAGE
PRODare the keys.
And:
"https://dev.app.com"
"https://stage.app.com"
"https://app.com"are the values.
Unlike arrays, plain JavaScript objects do not have array methods such as:
.forEach()
.map()
.filter()You cannot write:
envUrls.forEach(...);because envUrls is an object, not an array.
JavaScript provides three powerful static methods that extract object data into arrays:

• Object.keys()

• Object.values()

• Object.entries()
Once the data is represented as an array, you can use familiar array methods to process it.

The Three Core Object Methods
MethodWhat It DoesWhat It ReturnsObject.keys(obj)Extracts the object's property namesAn array of keysObject.values(obj)Extracts the object's property valuesAn array of valuesObject.entries(obj)Extracts both keys and valuesAn array of [key, value] pairs
A simple mental model is:

• Object.keys() → Give me the property names.

• Object.values() → Give me the property values.

• Object.entries() → Give me both.
Let’s examine each method using QA automation examples.

Our Example Environment Configuration
Assume your automation framework stores environment URLs in an object:
const envUrls = {
DEV: "https://dev.app.com",
STAGE: "https://stage.app.com",
PROD: "https://app.com"
};This object contains three key-value pairs.
DEV → https://dev.app.com
STAGE → https://stage.app.com
PROD → https://app.comDepending on the testing requirement, you may need only the environment names, only the URLs, or both.

Object.keys(): Get the Property Names
Object.keys() extracts the object's own enumerable property names and returns them as an array.
const environments =
Object.keys(envUrls);console.log(environments);Output:
[
"DEV",
"STAGE",
"PROD"
]The original object:
{
DEV: "https://dev.app.com",
STAGE: "https://stage.app.com",
PROD: "https://app.com"
}has been transformed into an array containing only the keys:
[
"DEV",
"STAGE",
"PROD"
]Because the result is an array, you can now use array methods.
For example:
const environments =
Object.keys(envUrls);environments.forEach(environment => {
console.log(environment);
});Output:
DEV
STAGE
PROD
QA Application: Check Whether a Configuration Key Exists
Suppose your automation framework expects a STAGE environment.
You can write:
const environments =
Object.keys(envUrls);const hasStageEnvironment =
environments.includes("STAGE");console.log(hasStageEnvironment);Output:
trueIn a test:
expect(
Object.keys(envUrls)
).toContain("STAGE");This verifies that the required configuration key exists.
For a direct property-existence check, modern JavaScript also provides:
Object.hasOwn(envUrls, "STAGE");which returns:
trueSo the right approach depends on the requirement.
Use Object.keys() when you need the collection of keys.
Use Object.hasOwn() when you only need to check whether one own property exists.

QA Application: Count Configuration Fields
Because Object.keys() returns an array, you can use .length.
const environmentCount =
Object.keys(envUrls).length;console.log(environmentCount);Output:
3This can be useful when validating the expected structure of configuration or API data.
Example:
const user = {
id: 101,
name: "Ravi",
role: "QA"
};expect(
Object.keys(user).length
).toBe(3);
Object.values(): Get the Property Values
Object.values() extracts the values from an object and returns them as an array.
const urls =
Object.values(envUrls);console.log(urls);Output:
[
"https://dev.app.com",
"https://stage.app.com",
"https://app.com"
]The keys are ignored.
You receive only the values.

QA Application: Validate Allowed Values
Suppose an API response contains user roles:
const roleConfig = {
ADMIN: "Admin",
QA: "Quality Engineer",
DEV: "Developer"
};You can extract all allowed role values:
const allowedRoles =
Object.values(roleConfig);Result:
[
"Admin",
"Quality Engineer",
"Developer"
]Now validate an API response:
const actualRole =
"Quality Engineer";expect(
allowedRoles
).toContain(actualRole);This is useful when the property names themselves are irrelevant and you only care about the actual data values.

QA Application: Validate Every Configuration Value
Suppose you want to verify that every configured URL uses HTTPS.
const urls =
Object.values(envUrls);const invalidUrls = urls.filter(
url => !url.startsWith("https://")
);Then:
expect(invalidUrls).toEqual([]);If all URLs begin with:
https://the test passes.
If one value is:
http://dev.app.comthe invalidUrls array will contain it, exposing the configuration problem.

Object.entries(): Get Keys and Values Together
Object.entries() extracts both the key and the value.
Consider:
const entries =
Object.entries(envUrls);console.log(entries);Output:
[
[
"DEV",
"https://dev.app.com"
],
[
"STAGE",
"https://stage.app.com"
],
[
"PROD",
"https://app.com"
]
]Each property becomes a two-item array:
[
key,
value
]For example:
[
"DEV",
"https://dev.app.com"
]This is why Object.entries() is especially useful when you need to process both the property name and its value.

Understanding the Nested Array Structure
The result of:
Object.entries(envUrls);is an array containing smaller arrays.
Conceptually:
[
["DEV", "https://dev.app.com"],
["STAGE", "https://stage.app.com"],
["PROD", "https://app.com"]
]The outer array contains all entries.
Each inner array contains exactly two values:
[key, value]That structure works naturally with array destructuring.

Combining Object.entries() with Array Destructuring
You could loop through the entries like this:
Object.entries(envUrls).forEach(entry => {
const envName = entry[0];
const url = entry[1]; console.log(envName, url);
});This works, but it is unnecessarily verbose.
Because each entry has the structure:
[envName, url]you can destructure it directly:
Object.entries(envUrls).forEach(
([envName, url]) => {
console.log(envName, url);
}
);The syntax:
[envName, url]means:
Take the first array value and store it in envName, then take the second value and store it in url.
For:
[
"DEV",
"https://dev.app.com"
]JavaScript assigns:
envName = "DEV";
url = "https://dev.app.com";This happens automatically for every entry.

QA Application: Automated Environment Health Check
Imagine your automation framework contains:
const envUrls = {
DEV: "https://dev.app.com",
STAGE: "https://stage.app.com",
PROD: "https://app.com"
};Before running the test suite, you want to verify that every environment is reachable.
First, convert the object into key-value pairs:
const environmentPairs =
Object.entries(envUrls);The result is:
[
["DEV", "https://dev.app.com"],
["STAGE", "https://stage.app.com"],
["PROD", "https://app.com"]
]For simple synchronous logging, you can use .forEach():
environmentPairs.forEach(
([envName, url]) => {
console.log(
`[HEALTH CHECK] Pinging ${envName} environment at: ${url}`
);
}
);Output:
[HEALTH CHECK] Pinging DEV environment at: https://dev.app.com
[HEALTH CHECK] Pinging STAGE environment at: https://stage.app.com
[HEALTH CHECK] Pinging PROD environment at: https://app.comThis demonstrates the combination of:

• Object.entries()

• Array iteration

• Array destructuring

• Template literals

A Critical Async Correction for Real API Health Checks
Logging inside .forEach() is fine because console.log() is synchronous.
However, a real health check usually contains asynchronous operations:
await request.get(url);You should not write:
Object.entries(envUrls).forEach(
async ([envName, url]) => {
const response =
await request.get(url); expect(
response.status()
).toBe(200);
}
);The problem is that .forEach() does not wait for asynchronous callbacks to complete.
Your setup code may continue before all health checks have finished.
For sequential health checks, use for...of.
for (
const [envName, url]
of Object.entries(envUrls)
) {
console.log(
`[HEALTH CHECK] Pinging ${envName} environment at: ${url}`
); const response =
await request.get(url); expect(
response.status()
).toBe(200);
}Now each request completes before the next iteration begins.

Complete Sequential Health Check Example
A realistic Playwright-style example could look like this:
const envUrls = {
DEV: "https://dev.app.com",
STAGE: "https://stage.app.com",
PROD: "https://app.com"
};for (
const [envName, url]
of Object.entries(envUrls)
) {
console.log(
`[HEALTH CHECK] Checking ${envName}: ${url}`
); const response =
await request.get(url); const status =
response.status(); console.log(
`[HEALTH CHECK] ${envName} returned status ${status}`
); expect(status).toBe(200);
}The execution flow is:

• Extract the first [key, value] pair.

• Assign the key to envName.

• Assign the value to url.

• Send the HTTP request.

• Wait for the response.

• Validate the status.

• Move to the next environment.
This produces predictable sequential execution.

Parallel Health Checks with Promise.all()
Sometimes you do not need to check environments sequentially.
If the health checks are independent, you can execute them concurrently.
First, use Object.entries() to obtain the environment pairs.
Then use .map() to transform every pair into a Promise.
const healthChecks =
Object.entries(envUrls).map(
async ([envName, url]) => {
console.log(
`[HEALTH CHECK] Checking ${envName}: ${url}`
); const response =
await request.get(url); return {
envName,
url,
status: response.status()
};
}
);Now:
healthChecksis an array of Promises.
Wait for all of them:
const results =
await Promise.all(healthChecks);Then validate the results:
results.forEach(result => {
expect(result.status).toBe(200);
});The complete example is:
const results = await Promise.all(
Object.entries(envUrls).map(
async ([envName, url]) => {
const response =
await request.get(url); return {
envName,
url,
status: response.status()
};
}
)
);results.forEach(result => {
expect(result.status).toBe(200);
});This pattern is useful when:

• The requests are independent.

• Execution order does not matter.

• Running them concurrently is safe.

• You want faster execution.

Sequential vs Parallel Execution
Use for...of when:

• Each operation must finish before the next begins.

• Execution order matters.

• Requests depend on previous results.

• You want simpler step-by-step debugging.
Example:
for (
const [envName, url]
of Object.entries(envUrls)
) {
await checkEnvironment(
envName,
url
);
}Use Promise.all() when:

• Operations are independent.

• Execution order does not matter.

• Parallel execution is safe.

• Faster execution is desirable.
Example:
await Promise.all(
Object.entries(envUrls).map(
([envName, url]) =>
checkEnvironment(
envName,
url
)
)
);This distinction matters when converting object data into asynchronous automation workflows.

Combining Object Methods with Array Methods
The real power of these methods appears when you combine them with array operations.

Object.keys() + .includes()
Question:
Does this configuration contain the PROD environment?
const hasProd =
Object.keys(envUrls)
.includes("PROD");Result:
true
Object.values() + .filter()
Question:
Which URLs do not use HTTPS?
const insecureUrls =
Object.values(envUrls)
.filter(
url =>
!url.startsWith(
"https://"
)
);Result:
[]An empty array means no insecure URLs were found.

Object.entries() + .filter()
Question:
Which environments use a staging URL?
const stagingEntries =
Object.entries(envUrls)
.filter(
([envName, url]) =>
url.includes("stage")
);Result:
[
[
"STAGE",
"https://stage.app.com"
]
]
Object.entries() + .map()
Question:
Convert the environment configuration into readable log messages.
const environmentMessages =
Object.entries(envUrls)
.map(
([envName, url]) =>
`${envName}: ${u
